title: Apprenticeship Pathways Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Apprenticeship Pathways Act of 2025 creates a new program run by the Department of Labor. This program gives money to organizations that help high school students find apprenticeships in important jobs like trades, healthcare, and technology. The program focuses especially on helping students from poor schools, rural areas, and other groups that need extra support. The goal is to help more young people learn valuable job skills while they are still in high school.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Apprenticeship Pathways Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these words mean the following. An apprenticeship is a job training program that is officially registered under a national law called the National Apprenticeship Act. Disability has the same meaning as in the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990. Disconnected youth means any person who is between 16 and 25 years old, has not been regularly attending high school or college in the past six months, and has not had a regular job during those same six months. Early college high school means a special type of high school as defined in federal education law. Educational service agency has the meaning given in federal education law. High school has the meaning given in federal education law. Industry intermediary means an organization that connects employers with government agencies and schools to help create new apprenticeship programs for high school students. These organizations can be businesses, nonprofit groups that work with businesses, private groups that help match workers with jobs, or groups made up of any of these types of organizations. Local educational agency has the meaning given in federal education law. National Apprenticeship Act means a federal law from 1937 that governs apprenticeship programs. Pre-apprenticeship program means a training program that prepares people to enter an apprenticeship program and meets federal standards. Region has the meaning given in the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act. Secondary school has the meaning given in federal education law. STEM means science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.

sections.title: Section 3 Secondary Education Apprenticeship Contracts
sections.text: The Secretary of Labor, working with the Secretary of Education, must make contracts with industry intermediary organizations. The purpose is to help create more apprenticeship opportunities for high school students. To get a contract, an industry intermediary must submit an application that describes what activities they plan to do to help high school students get apprenticeships. The Secretary of Labor will choose which organizations get contracts based on competitive standards. The Secretary will give priority to organizations that focus on helping students from schools where at least 40 percent of students qualify for free or reduced-price lunches, students in rural areas that need extra help, students living in high-poverty areas, disconnected youth, Native American children, women entering building trades and technology, and people with disabilities. Organizations that get contracts can use the money for many activities including helping develop apprenticeships and pre-apprenticeships, encouraging employers to create these programs, finding and training students for apprenticeships, tracking student progress, providing support and coaching, recruiting employers, paying for administrative costs, providing wraparound support services like tutoring and transportation help, and paying half of an apprentice's wages during their apprenticeship. They can also buy goods and services like educational materials, equipment and tools, work clothing and safety gear, and technology and internet access. The apprenticeships must be in high-need occupations including building trades like carpentry, plumbing, welding, and electrical work, architectural and engineering drafting, health care jobs like emergency medical technicians and pharmacy technicians, teaching including early childhood education, technology jobs like computer science and data science, and manufacturing. Congress is authorized to provide whatever money is necessary to carry out this program.
